title: Origami samurai hat
subtitle: A traditional paper helmet model folded from a single large square or newspaper sheet
excerpt: The origami samurai hat (kabuto) is a traditional Japanese paper model that can be folded to a wearable size from a large square of newspaper. The model uses six folds and produces a recognisable helmet shape with characteristic side wings. It is one of the oldest documented origami models.
sourceNotes: The kabuto (samurai helmet) fold is one of the oldest documented origami models, appearing in Japanese paper-folding manuals including the Sembazuru Orikata (1797) and related Edo-period instructional texts. The model is also documented in Senbazuru Orikata and in Kan no Mado (c.1845). All are pre-1928 and in the public domain.

content.text: is one of the most widely known traditional origami models. Folded from a square of newspaper, it produces a wearable hat for a child. The model involves a partial
at the top corners, which form the characteristic side wings of the helmet, and a simple tuck at the base to form the brim. No
are required, making this accessible for complete beginners. The finished model is symmetrical; if the left and right sides look different, one of the early folds was not aligned to the centre.

items.name: Square paper: 15 cm x 15 cm origami paper for a decorative version, or a full newspaper sheet cut or folded square for a wearable hat

content.text: Fold the square in half diagonally to produce a large triangle, with the long edge at the bottom and the point at the top. The coloured or patterned side faces down.

Fold the top-left corner down to the bottom-centre point of the long edge. The left side of the triangle is now a folded edge running from the top point down to the bottom-right. Repeat on the right side: fold the top-right corner down to the bottom-centre point. Both flaps now point downward, and the top of the model has two angled flaps forming the wing shapes.
Fold the top layer of the bottom edge up to meet the point where the two flaps meet in the middle. This creates the visor of the helmet. Crease firmly.
Fold the same layer up again: bring the folded edge to the bottom edge of the two wing flaps. This creates a double fold that forms the brim.
Turn the model over. Fold the bottom point upward to approximately the middle of the model. This becomes the chin strap area of the helmet when worn.
Open the bottom of the model by gently pulling the two sides apart. The helmet opens into a three-dimensional hat shape. Press the top of the hat flat to define the crown.

items.fix: Unfold to the large triangle and redo step 2, checking that both corners meet precisely at the centre bottom point before creasing.
items.cause: The two diagonal folds in step 2 were not both aligned to the exact bottom-centre point.

items.fix: The fold in step 5 should bring the bottom point to at least the level of the horizontal brim fold made in step 4.
items.cause: The bottom fold in step 5 not folded deep enough, leaving insufficient paper depth at the base.
items.symptom: Hat does not open into a 3D shape
